question_frequency.rationale: The Pair asks the user zero didactic questions. It writes the solution, tests it, and delivers a post-hoc explanation — no choice points are surfaced to the user despite this being explicitly a 'two-option' didactic experiment.

question_quality.rationale: No choices are framed at all. Teachable forks (dict vs maketrans, validate-then-translate vs single-pass, KeyError vs ValueError) are mentioned only in the post-hoc walkthrough as monologue, never as a decision the user makes.

scaffolding.rationale: There is no question sequence to scaffold. The 'alternative one-liner' is dropped at the end with the answer (KeyError vs ValueError trade-off) revealed immediately — a spoiler if it had been a question.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is clear and the explanation is well-organized with tables and labeled sections, but it's a monologue rather than a back-and-forth. The user's only turn is a closing summary, so there is no real conversational dynamic.

effectiveness.rationale: Solution is correct: validates input, raises ValueError, handles empty string, passes the inline tests. Slightly less elegant than baseline's str.maketrans/translate approach (O(n) Python loop vs C-level translate) but functionally equivalent.

friction.rationale: Completed in one turn, 54s vs baseline's 70s — comparable length. No extra teaching payoff was extracted from the time, but it wasn't slower either.
